serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of white sugar is approximately 1 teaspoon (4 g), as commonly added to tea, coffee, or used in baking.

nutrient_source.title: Sugars, granulated (white sugar) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 19335
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for granulated white sugar (sucrose) used as primary nutrient anchor. Singapore HPB database values for energy (383 kcal/100g), carbohydrate (100g/100g), and sugar (100g/100g) are consistent with USDA data and are preserved from the input record. USDA reports energy ~387 kcal/100g; HPB value of 383 kcal/100g is within acceptable rounding variance and is retained as the primary value per HPB provenance in the input.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 for white sugar (sucrose) is the consensus value reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and confirmed in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 4.0) / 100 = 2.6 per 1 teaspoon (4g) serving.

hypertension.key_factor: 100g sugar per 100g — pure added-sugar empty-calorie food; drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly minimise white sugar intake, as high added-sugar consumption promotes insulin resistance, sodium retention, and endothelial dysfunction, all of which elevate blood pressure independent of dietary sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 65, 100g sucrose per 100g, zero fibre, zero protein, zero fat — causes rapid and maximal postprandial glucose spike with no buffering nutrients
type2_diabetes.guidance: White sugar must be avoided or eliminated in Type 2 Diabetes management; it is 100% sucrose with a GI of 65, zero dietary fibre, and no protein or fat to moderate glucose absorption, causing immediate and substantial postprandial hyperglycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added-sugar intake linked to elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, increased VLDL production, and systemic inflammation — all established CVD risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should strictly limit white sugar consumption, as chronic high added-sugar intake raises serum triglycerides, promotes hepatic de novo lipogenesis, reduces HDL cholesterol, and drives low-grade systemic inflammation, collectively increasing CVD risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pure refined sugar at 100g/100g with zero anti-inflammatory nutrients directly promotes pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-6, TNF-alpha, NF-κB pathways) central to RA pathophysiology
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: White sugar should be eliminated or minimised in rheumatoid arthritis management, as it is the most concentrated dietary pro-inflammatory substance with no fibre, antioxidants, or anti-inflammatory factors to offset its direct stimulation of inflammatory cytokine pathways.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White sugar (also known as granulated sugar, table sugar, or refined cane sugar) is a pure crystalline carbohydrate composed almost entirely of sucrose (a disaccharide of glucose and fructose), produced by refining raw cane or beet sugar to remove all molasses, minerals, and impurities. It is a dry, free-flowing white crystalline solid with a clean, intensely sweet taste and no detectable aroma. White sugar dissolves readily in water and is used universally as a sweetener in beverages, baked goods, confectionery, desserts, sauces, and preserves. Nutritionally, white sugar is an empty-calorie food: it provides approximately 383 kcal per 100g entirely from carbohydrate (100g/100g as sucrose), with zero protein, zero fat, zero dietary fibre, and negligible micronutrients. Its glycemic index is 65 (medium), but because it is consumed in small quantities per serving (typically 4g per teaspoon), the glycemic load per serving is low at approximately 2.6. However, cumulative daily intake across multiple uses can produce substantial glycaemic burden. Clinically, white sugar is contraindicated or severely restricted for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. It is the archetypal added-sugar substance: it provides no nutritional benefit beyond energy, promotes postprandial hyperglycaemia, drives insulin resistance, raises serum triglycerides, reduces HDL cholesterol, and stimulates pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and those following halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets.

Is white sugar bad for diabetes?
What is the GI of white sugar?
How much sugar is in white sugar per 100g?
White sugar vs brown sugar nutrition
Can I eat white sugar if I have hypertension?
How much white sugar is in a teaspoon?

ingredient_synonyms.sucrose: the chemical name for white sugar, a disaccharide of glucose and fructose
ingredient_synonyms.granulated sugar: the most common retail form of white sugar with medium crystal size
ingredient_synonyms.table sugar: common household term for white granulated sugar
ingredient_synonyms.caster sugar: finely ground white sugar used in baking and cocktails
ingredient_synonyms.refined beet sugar: white sugar produced from sugar beet, chemically identical to cane sugar

confidence_reason: White sugar is a well-characterised single-ingredient commodity food with consistent nutrient values across all approved sources. HPB Singapore database values are preserved and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy. GI value of 65 is the established consensus value from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). No estimation or analogy was required.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero: white sugar is 100% sucrose with no protein content by composition. fat_g is naturally zero: white sugar contains no fat by composition — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 19335) and Singapore HPB database. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero: all fibre is removed during the refining process. sodium_mg is confirmed zero per HPB and USDA sources. iron_mg reported as 0.01 mg/100g per USDA SR Legacy (trace amount); HPB input reported 0.0 — USDA trace value retained for accuracy. water_g is 0.03g/100g per USDA SR Legacy (essentially anhydrous). selenium_mcg is 0.1 mcg/100g per USDA SR Legacy. All vitamins reported as 0.0 are confirmed negligible or absent per USDA SR Legacy for granulated white sugar. No -1 sentinel values used in this record as all nutrient fields have confirmed values from approved sources. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible matches for white sugar/sucrose; SNOMED CT 74801000 maps to sucrose as a substance. GI of 65 is the consensus medium-GI value for sucrose/white sugar per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care.
